what are achieved and ascribed statuses? what are achieved and ascribed statuses? what are achieved and ascribed statuses? what are achieved and ascribed statuses? what are achieved and ascribed statuses? In sociology, achieved status and ascribed status are two distinct concepts that describe the ways in which individuals acquire social positions or roles. Achieved status refers to the social position or role that an individual has obtained through their own efforts, abilities, and choices. This means that an individual has earned their status through their hard work, education, skills, or other personal attributes. Examples of achieved status include a person becoming a doctor or lawyer after completing medical school or law school. On the other hand, ascribed status refers to the social position or role that an individual is assigned at birth or acquired through circumstances beyond their control. This means that an individual's status is determined by factors such as their family background, socioeconomic class, ethnicity, or geographic location.
